summary: Barn‑burner Lovelight transition, soaring Good Lovin’, acoustic gems
blurb: The set opens with a tight acoustic half that includes Ripple, Brokedown Palace and the first‑time How Long Blues. The electric side erupts into a barn‑burning Turn On Your Lovelight segue and an ecstatic Good Lovin’ jam featuring Jerry’s pedal‑steel work. Overall the band is locked in and the show stands out as a highlight of the ’70 Fillmore run.
review: On August 19, 1970 at the Fillmore West the Dead delivered one of their most exhilarating nights of the era. The acoustic opener shines – Pigpen’s rare piano underpins a beautiful Ripple‑Brokedown Palace pair and a debut How Long Blues that sets a warm, intimate tone. When the band flips to electric they explode with a barn‑burning Turn On Your Lovelight transition from Not Fade Away, and Good Lovin’ roars out of the stage, propelled by Jerry’s soaring pedal‑steel guitar and crisp, defined bass from Phil. Highlights like China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider and the seamless flow into Saint Stephen keep the momentum high, while New Speedway Boogie showcases a fiery solo that caps an unforgettable second set. The Taback audience cassette (gd1970-08-19.aud.taback.cdjones.81775.sbeok.flac16) captures this energy with surprisingly clean mic placement and lively bass definition, making it the recommended listening source despite the vintage tape’s occasional rough edges.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: One recording is clearly positive and the other two are mixed, giving an overall moderate positive consensus. Multiple recordings praise the same standout moments – Lovelight transition, Good Lovin’, China Cat Sunflower, and acoustic highlights – indicating consistent enthusiasm without significant negative commentary.

best_recording.reason: High‑quality audience cassette with crisp mic placement, clear instrument separation and faithful capture of both acoustic and electric halves; widely praised as the cleanest source for this show.

key_highlights: Acoustic opener featuring Ripple, Brokedown Palace and first‑time How Long Blues
Barn‑burner Turn On Your Lovelight transition from Not Fade Away
Ecstatic Good Lovin’ jam with Jerry’s pedal‑steel solo and tight Phil bass
Seamless China Cat Sunflower → I Know You Rider flow and energetic Saint Stephen

band_performance.Jerry: Garcia delivers a stellar guitar tone, adds pedal‑steel flavor on Good Lovin’ and New Speedway Boogie, and strings together soaring solos throughout the night.
band_performance.Phil: Lesh’s bass is lively, well‑defined and pushes forward even through the audience mix, anchoring both acoustic and electric sets.
band_performance.Bob: Weir provides solid rhythm work and a standout solo on New Minglewood Blues, contributing reliably to the band’s groove.
band_performance.Pigpen: His piano on the acoustic set offers rare moments of melodic depth; while organ is less audible on the electric half, his presence adds a warm texture.
